contest.title: 2025-2026 ICPC Asia Taichung Regional Contest (Unrated, Online Mirror, ICPC Rules, Teams Preferred)

sources.notes: Choose a cut before repeated perfect riffle shuffles to get the lexicographically smallest final deck.
Writeup/code uses the permutation structure of perfect riffles and rolling-hash comparison of candidate rotations.
Alternative explanation groups positions into blocks after t mod k riffles and compares candidates by hashing.

annotation.summary: Given a deck of size 2^k, choose a cyclic cut so that after t perfect riffle shuffles the final string is lexicographically minimum.
annotation.constraints: n is large but power-of-two structured; all possible cuts cannot be compared character-by-character.
annotation.core_idea: The perfect riffle acts as a bit rotation/permutation on positions and has period k, so only t mod k matters. Precompute where each original position moves. Then compare final strings from different cuts using rolling hashes and binary-search/LCP, or maintain block hashes implied by the permutation structure, to find the lexicographically smallest cut.
annotation.complexity: O(n log n) with rolling-hash comparisons.

solution_variants.name: permutation-hash-min-rotation
solution_variants.summary: Reduce repeated riffles to a fixed permutation and use hashes to compare all cyclic cuts.

tags.evidence: Candidate cuts are compared by rolling-hash/LCP queries instead of direct O(n) comparison.
The task is essentially lexicographic comparison of many suffix/rotation-like strings.
LCP between two candidate cuts can be found by binary searching the first differing position with hashes.
The key reduction is recognizing the riffle permutation as periodic/bit-rotational.
tags.description: Exploit a special permutation form or period to avoid simulating repeated operations.
tags.created_reason: Permutation-structure reductions recur in constructive and string transformation tasks.
